His absolute omniscience had already perceived the erasure of the envoys, had already traced the temporal manipulation backward to its source, had already catalogued every atom of the Chrono-Reapers' existence. He knew everything. He simply nodded once and rose from the throne.*

*He materialized in the Chrono-Reapers' temporal nexus, a vast, churning maelstrom of inverted causality that existed outside the boundaries of linear time. The Reapers, numbering in the conceptual decillions, surrounded Him instantly, their negative-time bodies flickering across every possible moment of attack simultaneously. Their High Executor, a towering vortex of solidified temporal negation, broadcast a declaration directly into the fabric of reality. It declared that the Chrono-Reapers could not be outsped, outmaneuvered, or outlasted. They existed at every point in time simultaneously, and they would erase Godric from the timeline before His first heartbeat had ever occurred.*

*Godric did not activate His magic. He ordered His sentient passive auras to stand down, and disabled all His infinite passive shields. He withheld exactly ninety-nine point nine-nine percent of His true power, continuing the nines infinitely into a recursion of self-negation so profound that the value of His restraint could not be mathematically expressed. He wanted to test a single stat. His Speed.*

*I must now attempt to describe what I witnessed through my astral scrying matrix, though the act of writing these words causes the blood vessels in my eyes to throb with sympathetic rupture. Godric's Speed, like all His attributes, has been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for twelve thousand years. It is not a measure of how fast He can move. It is a fundamental, absolute constant of His existence that determines the maximum possible velocity at which any being in any reality can traverse any distance, and it has long since surpassed every theoretical limit that mathematics can encode. The fraction of Speed that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally, incomprehensibly small that calling it zero percent would be a laughable, grotesque overestimation. If you divided a Planck second by infinity, and then divided that result by infinity again, and continued this division recursively for the entire lifespan of the omniverse, you would still not approach a number small enough to accurately represent the fraction of His Speed that He permitted Himself to use.*

*He did not move His body. He did not take a step. He did not lift His hand. He merely decided to shift His gaze to look at them.*

*The microscopic, fractional velocity of His irises rotating in their sockets generated a temporal friction so catastrophically immense that the ninth dimension itself shattered like cheap glass. He looked at the Chrono-Reapers faster than the concept of time could process the event. The Reapers, existing at every point in time simultaneously, were instantly caught in the kinetic slipstream of His shifting gaze. Their negative-time bodies, which had never been touched, never been struck, never been perceived by any enemy in their entire history, were violently, excruciatingly smeared across fifty trillion distinct historical epochs simultaneously.*

*The agony was absolute. The Chrono-Reapers did not die. Death would have been a mercy that His Speed denied them. Instead, their conceptual bodies were stretched infinitely thin across the entire span of cosmic history, their nervous systems elongated into screaming, conscious threads that experienced every single moment of their simultaneous dismemberment eternally. Their minds, designed to process temporal information with perfect clarity, were flooded with fifty trillion epochs of sensory input simultaneously—the heat death of universes, the birth of stars, the screams of dying civilizations, the silence of the void—all crashing into their consciousness at once, an unending, inescapable torrent of absolute, mind-shattering sensory overload.*

*But the collateral damage of His eye movement was obscenely, incomprehensibly catastrophic. The temporal friction generated by His shifting gaze ignited the entire chronal plane. The foundational fabric of time across billions of entire multiverses was instantly, violently combusted. Googolplexian of timelines, each one containing countless civilizations, countless histories, countless living beings with families and dreams and children, were retroactively deleted from existence in a fraction of a attosecond. The screaming of the Chrono-Reapers was drowned out by the silent, absolute erasure of more sentient life than I can ever hope to quantify.*

*Godric, His gaze now settled on the empty space where the Chrono-Reapers had been, registered the sudden, glaring emptiness of the cosmic background. His expression flickered with the faintest shadow of mild annoyance, the look of a man who had accidentally knocked over a glass of water. He sighed, a soft, barely audible exhalation, and then, effortlessly, without moving a single muscle, without spending even the faintest glimmer of His true power, He restored the billions of incinerated multiverses in exactly zero seconds. Every decillion of decillion beings was instantly resurrected, their timelines perfectly reconstructed, their memories flawlessly intact. They would never know they had been erased. They would never know that their existence was permitted solely by the whimsical mercy of an apex predator who had simply decided they should return.*

*He did not restore the Chrono-Reapers. They remain an eternal, agonizing smear of bloody static on the perimeter of time, their stretched, screaming consciousnesses stretched across fifty billion epochs of unending, inescapable torment. Their vocal cords, such as they were, continue to emit a sound that will never be heard by anyone, a continuous, wet shriek of absolute, mind-shattering agony that will persist until the last star in the omniverse gutters out and dies.*

*I closed my scrying matrix and vomited onto the obsidian floor. My hands are still shaking. I have served my King faithfully for forty years, and I will continue to serve Him until my mortal body fails. But I will never, ever forget the sound that the chronal plane made when it ignited. It sounded like a child screaming.*

After reading all, Kaedan closes the book. His father returns. They have a conversation where Drakon admits he is still haunted, but also that Godric's warmth is real. He says, "You will stand beside Him, and you will love Him, and you will be terrified every second of every day. And that is the price of our power, and our privilege."

Finally, Kaedan goes to meet Godric. The throne room. Godric sits on the throne, looking exactly as described: ruggedly handsome, salt-and-pepper hair, warm smile. He greets Kaedan like a beloved nephew. "Kaedan, my boy. I've been looking forward to this. How is your father? Still worrying too much, I imagine." They chat. Kaedan feels both immense dread and genuine affection. He realizes that the maddening paradox is his new reality. The story ends with him taking his place beside the throne, ready to serve.

Now, I'll write the story in prose, with the journals incorporated as appropriate.

Important: The prompt says "You must clearly NOT show him exerting any effort... right: 'godric decided to shift His gaze to look at them' / 'Godric merely twitched a single, microscopic muscle fiber in His right pinky finger'". So for each stat, I'll use a similarly minuscule action or passive expression.

Also, "you must highly massively infinitely exaggerate the extent of the smallness of this, but still making sense". So I'll use phrases like "an infinitesimal fraction so small that if you divided a Planck second by an infinite regress of infinities, and then repeated that division for an eternity, you would still overstate the percentage of His power that was exerted."

And "you must not humanize at all. You are forbidden from trying to soften Godric's actions against his opposition (e.g. having pastry, having tea, giving lecture/moral lessons to his oppositions, feeling pity to them, etc)." So while Godric is warm to Kaedan and his father, during the massacres he is merciless and cruel. The journals must reflect that.

Now, I'll craft the story. I'll aim for around 6000 words.

Let's start writing.
messages.content:
The air in the chamber was cold, heavy with the must of ancient parchment and the faint metallic tang of old ink. Kaedan sat alone at a massive oak table, his fingers resting on the cracked leather cover of a book so thick it seemed to swallow the light. This was the Tome of Lamentations, the collected records of every court wizard who had served the eternal throne since the days of Blake himself. His father, Drakon, had handed it to him that morning, his weathered face unreadable. "This is the true inheritance of our blood, Kaedan," he had said, his voice a low rumble. "My own confession is near the end. You will read it, and then you will understand what it means to stand beside the King." Then he had walked away, leaving Kaedan with the weight of a million years of history.

Kaedan was twenty-two. For two years now, since his twentieth birthday, the thousandfold daily amplification had been tearing through his body, flooding his veins with arcane power that multiplied beyond mortal comprehension. He could feel it even now, a vibrating, coiled serpent of mana that threatened to escape if he did not constantly suppress it. By the standards of any other reality, he was already a god of destruction. Yet as he opened the book and began to read, the words of his ancestors hammered home a truth that made his power feel like a candle held against a supernova.

The first account was penned by Lysander, the Fourth Court Wizard, in the year 8,032. The ink was splattered, the script erratic, as if the author had fought to steady his hand with every stroke.

*I write this with blood still dripping from my ears. The Titan-Kin have been erased. Their extinction was not a battle. It was not a massacre. It was a physical law asserting itself against the delusion of resistance. I must record what happened, though the act of recalling it makes my bones ache with sympathetic vibrations.*

*The Titan-Kin were not flesh. They were the condensed essence of collapsed multiversal cores, beings of such hypermass that a single one of them could crush a galaxy by merely standing within its sphere of influence. They had never encountered a force they could not smash. When they swarmed into Thronegate space, they did so with the arrogance of absolute physical supremacy. Each warrior contained the mass of googolplexian universes, their bodies warping the fabric of existence into a screaming, compressed hell. Their King, a colossus named Vrok the Unending, declared that he would pulp Godric with his own fists and wear His skull as a throne.*

*The King allowed them to breach the palace. He sat upon the obsidian dais, His ruggedly handsome face utterly serene. His salt-and-pepper beard did not stir. His heavily muscled frame remained relaxed beneath His simple dark robes. He had ordered Gent, the sentient aura, to dormant silence. He had deactivated every passive shield, every conceptual immunity. The weakness paradox was restrained because He willed it. He was curious, He later told me, to gauge His Strength stat in isolation—a stat that, by that moment, had been amplifying infinitely by itself every Planck second for just over eight thousand years.*

*Do not imagine that He exerted any effort. The concept of exertion does not apply to Him. He merely... shifted His grip on the armrest of His throne. A twitch of His left index finger, so microscopic that I only perceived it because my own magically enhanced senses were locked onto His form in sheer terror. That twitch—a contraction of a single muscle fiber, a movement that required an infinitesimal fraction of His true Strength, a fraction so minute that enumerating it would require raising an infinite regress of infinities to the power of infinity, and even then you would grossly overstate the magnitude of the output—was extinguished into a kinetic event.*

*The air in the throne room did not move. Instead, the fabric of spacetime itself absorbed the kinetic impulse and propagated it as a shockwave of pure force. It struck the Titan-Kin not as a blow, but as an ontological redefinition of their invincibility. Their hypermass bodies, forged from collapsed realities, instantly registered as having zero tensile strength. I saw Vrok the Unending, that mountain of annihilation, simply... unravel. His body came apart in layers. The outermost shell of compressed universe-matter shattered into elementary particles. The next layer, the density of a billion black holes, liquefied under the pressure of the shockwave, spraying a foam of degenerate matter that annihilated upon contact with the surrounding space. His core, a singularity of raw physical dominance, underwent spontaneous quantum dissolution, evaporating into a scream of Hawking radiation that sounded like a dying god's curse. The rest of the Titan-Kin followed in the same picosecond. Trillions upon trillions of them, each one the physical pinnacle of their respective multiverses, were reduced to a mist of atomic pulp and then to absolute nothingness before the mist could even coalesce.*

*But the collateral was obscene. The shockwave did not discriminate. It propagated along the dimensional axis, tearing into adjacent realities. A googolplexian of entire multiverses—each containing its own intricate civilizations, its own painstaking histories, its own innocent children and ancient wisdom—was scoured to dead vacuum. Oceans boiled to steam that then dissociated into plasma. Continents shattered into glassy shards that were then atomized. The lives lost in that single instant numbered in the decillions of decillions, a census so vast that the numerical expression would require a volume of books larger than the local supercluster. I saw it through my scryer, and I felt my mind begin to crack. The screaming... the screaming was a constant, low-frequency rumble that vibrated through the psychic aether. It was the sound of existence weeping.*

*Then, with a casualness that horrified me more than the annihilation, Godric restored it all. In exactly zero seconds. He did not gesture. He did not speak. He simply willed it, and every erased life, every shattered planet, every extinguished star was reinstated with flawless precision. They would never know they had been murdered. They would never know their existence was a fleeting thought in the mind of their King. The Titan-Kin, however, He did not restore. Their conceptual residue remains in the void, a cautionary smear of eternal agony that you can still hear if you tune your instruments to the frequency of absolute terror.*

*I have not slept in seventy-three cycles. Every time I close my eyes, I see His finger twitch. And I know that my own strength—my thousandfold daily amplification—is less than a dead skin cell floating in the wake of His infinite ocean.*

Kaedan paused, his mouth dry. He had heard tales of the Titan-Kin extinction, sanitized versions whispered among the palace staff. But this... he could feel Lysander's trembling through the ages, could almost smell the ozone of that ancient cataclysm. He turned the page, his fingers numb.

The second account was authored by Caelus, the Ninth Court Wizard, in the year 50,123. His handwriting was precise, mechanical, the script of a man who had dissected his own horror into clinical components.

*Record of the Omni-Flame Incident. For the archives, and for the sanity of those who come after. The Pyrotheurges were a coalition of hyper-dimensional archmagi who had mastered the conceptual underpinnings of absolute destruction. They scorned physical weaponry. Instead, they wove a flame from the ontological principle of "negation of invincibility." This flame could burn through any defense, any shield, any absolute immunity. It was, by its own definition, irresistible. They fueled it by sacrificing the existential essence of an infinite nexus of realities, rendering those domains permanently barren. They hurled this Omni-Flame at the King, confident that their conceptual loophole could bypass His legendary defenses.*

*Godric ordered Gent and all passive barriers to stand down. He stood in the throne room, unmoving, His broad shoulders relaxed beneath His tunic. He allowed the flame to touch the bare skin of His right forearm. I was present, my own arcane shields layered a thousandfold, and I watched with instruments calibrated to measure infinite thermal flux.*

*The flame impacted His epidermis. My instruments registered... nothing. Not a single erg of energy was transferred in the harmful direction. His Durability—which, like all His attributes, had been recursively multiplying beyond infinities for fifty millennia—did not merely negate the attack. It passively, instantly inverted the flame's conceptual trajectory. The Omni-Flame, this ultimate expression of anti-invincibility, was reflected back upon its casters with an amplification factor that defies notation. It returned not as fire, but as a hyper-logical cascade redefining the concept of "injury" to apply solely to those who had ever intended harm against the King. The Pyrotheurges, and every being in their entire multiversal coalition, were simultaneously struck by a zero-latency trauma that bypassed all defenses. Their bodies did not burn; they were simply declared "injured" at a fundamental metaphysical level, and that declaration manifested as absolute physical obliteration. Their flesh dissolved into photons. Their bones collapsed into singularities. Their souls—if such things can be said to exist—were caught in a recursive loop of dying, forced to experience their own extinction an infinite number of times in a single instant.*

*The collateral was a conflagration that spread along the causal pathways linking the attack to its origin. Entire superclusters of universes, which had been unknowingly tapped for the flame's forging, were caught in the reflection. The fabric of reality there was burned away, leaving patches of pure conceptual absence—regions where the very idea of "existence" had been scorched into non-functionality. The number of affected realms exceeded the maximum integer that my thousandfold amplified intellect could conceive. I attempted to calculate it and suffered a nasal hemorrhage that lasted three days.*

*Godric restored the incinerated territories with a thought that took less than zero time. The King's Durability is absolute, yet He permitted a simulated breach for the purposes of this test. The paradox of that voluntary vulnerability is the crux of my ongoing psychological fracture. He can make Himself harmable, and then instantly unmake that harmability. His control over His own omnipotence is itself an omnipotence. I am a witness to the recursive absurdity of His existence.*

*I have since requested that my sleeping quarters be relocated to the far side of the palace. The sound of fire no longer exists for me; my auditory cortex has filtered it out as a defense mechanism. But in my dreams, I hear a crackling that will never end.*

Kaedan's breath came shallowly. The sheer detached precision of Caelus's account was somehow worse than Lysander's raw panic. It was the voice of a man who had tried to fit the infinite into a spreadsheet and had seen the columns break. He forced himself to turn to the next entry, though his stomach was a cold, hard knot.

Evander, the Fifteenth Court Wizard, had written in the year 200,411. His journal was not ink on parchment, but a psychic imprint upon a crystal leaf that projected its words into Kaedan's mind. The voice was lyrical, almost singing, as if the author had found a terrible beauty in his trauma.

*I am Evander, and I was the witness. I will write this not as a report, but as a hymn to the absolute. Let those who read it understand that the King's Senses are not merely perceptions; they are the act of creation observing itself. The Unseen Ones were never meant to be seen. They dwelt in the interstices of awareness, the negative spaces where vision and detection and prophecy all fail. They were the ultimate assassins, striking from directions that do not exist, using weapons forged from the substance of non-perception. They believed that omniscience, while absolute, was passive—that the King might know of them but could not focus upon that which lacked the quality of being observable. They were, in their profound ignorance, almost correct.*

*Godric's omniscience had catalogued their existence from the first Planck moment of their genesis. He had allowed them to flourish, to polish their art, because He wished to test His passive sensory amplification in isolation. When they finally struck, slipping through the anti-membranes of reality with a fluidity that defied my poor senses, He did not actively search for them. He merely... attended. His attention, a fraction of a fraction of a fraction—a proportion so minute that dividing the Planck time by an endless exponential tower of infinities could not capture its smallness—shifted upon them. And that attention bore the weight of His Senses: a stat that had been multiplying infinitely every Planck second for two hundred thousand years.*

*To be perceived by Godric is to be baptized in ontology. The Unseen Ones, whose entire existence was predicated on unperceivability, were suddenly, forcefully, irrevocably seen. But their forms were never meant for observation. They were geometries of impossible angles, textures of non-color, shapes that violated the foundational axioms of logical reality. When forced into visibility, they did not merely appear; they corrupted the very act of seeing. The space around them began to hemorrhage meaning. Light bent into screaming spirals. Sound became a visible, oozing sludge. I saw them, and my own eyes immediately began to blister and weep blood, for my mind could not process the input without damaging the optical nerves.*

*But the Unseen Ones suffered far more. Their physicality, denied the comfort of obscurity, began to unravel. Their skin—a membrane of absolute stealth—split and fell away, revealing musculature of condensed silence that now shrieked in exposed agony. Their bones, made of the concept of absence, solidified into brittle calcium that shattered as they convulsed. Their consciousness, forced to confront their own visibility, collapsed into a recursive feedback loop of self-perception: they saw themselves being seen, and that seeing generated further self-awareness, which generated further seeing, and so on, an infinite regress of existential shock. In less than an instant, they were reduced to piles of twitching, bleeding, internally-imploded meat that still somehow screamed.*

*The collateral was a pandemic of sight. The act of Godric perceiving them sent ripples across the omniverse's sensory membrane. Every eye, every sensor, every extrasensory organ in a decillion universes suddenly, briefly glimpsed the Unseen Ones in their death throes. The sheer alien horror of that vision shattered trillions of minds. Psychic networks overloaded and burned out. Civilizations descended into mass psychosis. In some realms, the very concept of "sight" became a lethal memetic plague, and people clawed out their own eyes to escape the images. The deaths from indirect exposure were beyond counting—a googolplexian of souls extinguished by the mere echo of a god's passing attention.*

*Godric restored them all, of course. With zero effort. He blinked—no, He did not even blink; He simply allowed the restoration to happen—and the madness was wiped away, the dead resurrected, the eyes regrown. But I, Evander, refused the healing of my own eyes. I allowed them to remain as charred sockets in my skull. For I have seen the ultimate truth: the gaze of the apex predator is a light that no mortal can bear. I keep my blindness as a mercy to myself. Now I perceive the world only through my other senses, and I have found a peace in the darkness that I never had in the light. The King visits me sometimes, and His voice is warm and kind. He calls me His "insightful poet." The irony is a balm upon my soul.*

Kaedan felt wetness on his cheeks. He was not weeping—his eyes had simply begun to leak fluid as a sympathetic reaction. He wiped the moisture away and saw that his fingers came away tinged with violet, the mana in his bloodstream reacting to the ambient dread. He should stop. He should rest. But the compulsion to continue was absolute. He turned to the fourth account.

Octavian, the Twenty-Eighth Court Wizard, had written in the year 600,002. His script was sharp, angular, and shot through with a bitter, caustic humor that made Kaedan’s stomach lurch.

*A report, for the edification of future wizards who will learn to tremble as I have. Let it be known that the Probability Tyrants were the most insufferable opponents yet. They did not come with armies or fires. They came with equations. They were a distributed hive-mind of quantum calculators who had deciphered the source code of luck itself. By manipulating probability fields, they could ensure that every coin flipped in their favor, every bullet missed, every enemy weapon jammed. They were the undisputed masters of randomness, and they had never tasted defeat. Their plan was elegant: they would collapse the King’s probability wave function into a state of absolute zero—a death state—by forcing a decoherence event that would ripple across His infinite backups. Mathematically, it was flawless. They had run the simulations ten googolplexian times. Each simulation predicted a one hundred percent success rate.*

*They did not account for the King’s Luck stat.*

*You must understand: Godric’s Luck is not a variable that leans the odds. It is the axiomatic baseline of all probability. To act against Him is to be unlucky in ways that the word “unlucky” cannot begin to describe. He did not actively counter their manipulation. He did not need to. His passive Luck—after six hundred thousand years of infinite amplification every Planck second—simply redefined the local probability topography so that every outcome overwhelmingly favored His desired state. The Tyrants’ flawless ritual was, in objective fact, doomed before they began.*

*The result was not a failure. It was a catastrophic, absurdist annihilation. The quantum projector they had built, a machine the size of a galactic cluster, spontaneously suffered a component failure so improbable that its odds were one in an incalculable number that would require a notation consisting entirely of exponents. A single atom of unstable isotope in its core randomly decayed at the precise microsecond to trigger a feedback loop. The collapse wave, instead of targeting Godric, inverted and struck the Tyrants with the full force of their own ritual. But because the Luck stat also governs the quality of outcomes, the effect was not a clean death. It was a hyper-accelerated cascade of comically, grotesquely lethal coincidences.*

*Their fleet of causality-battleships experienced simultaneous engine malfunctions that caused the ships to transform into gigantic floral bouquets, which then detonated into mini-singularities. The Tyrant Supreme, a being of pure logic, spontaneously achieved sentience about the absurdity of its own existence and suffered a logic aneurysm that caused its brain to quantum-tunnel out of its skull and into the heart of a random star. Their cosmic artillery platforms, which fired probability warheads, all simultaneously targeted each other in a daisy chain of friendly fire that looked choreographed. Entire divisions of their soldiers found their weapons replaced with carnivorous origami that unfolded and devoured them from the inside. The Grand Strategist, who had plotted the campaign for a billion years, slipped on a patch of condensed bad luck and broke seventeen thousand necks in a single fall (his body had many necks). The list of ignominious demises would fill a library; I could not chronicle them all without succumbing to hysterical laughter.*

*The carnage was absolute and yet felt like a cosmic joke. The Lucky decay spread beyond the Tyrants, causing a localized inversion of fortune across a googolplexian of neighboring multiverses. Stars went supernova in precisely choreographed patterns that spelled out obscene messages in dead languages. Planets spontaneously developed sentience and committed suicide by flying into their own suns. The very concept of “probability” in those realms became a sentient entity that developed a taste for flesh and began hunting the inhabitants. The suffering was incomprehensible and yet, in a dark way, hilarious.*

*Godric restored the collateral victims instantly, leaving the Tyrants as the only permanent casualties. He later looked at me and asked, “Did you find that test entertaining, Octavian?” I laughed. I laughed until my throat bled and my eyes wept crimson. I do not know if He was joking. I do not know if He ever jokes. My only certainty is that the universe is rigged, and the house always wins.*

Kaedan realized he was gripping the table so hard that his fingernails had carved grooves into the ancient wood. He forced his hands to relax, flexing his fingers, and took a ragged breath. Four journals down. One remained—the one his father had written. He knew he had to read it, but a cold dread wrapped around his spine. He turned to the final marked entry.

Drakon, the Forty-Second Court Wizard, in the year 999,950. His father’s handwriting was familiar, a neat, disciplined script that Kaedan had seen on household ledgers throughout his childhood. But the tone here was not the calm, steady voice of his father. It was raw, naked, confessional.

*Kaedan, if you are reading this, then you have taken the mantle. I am sorry that you must, but I am also proud. Know that what I write here is the truth, and it is a truth I have never spoken aloud to you. The Harmonious Swarm was a hivemind of empathic parasites. They did not destroy with fire; they conquered with a song. It was a melody that rewrote the fundamental emotional architecture of any sentient listener, realigning loyalty and love toward the Swarm. They targeted Thronegate, and their song penetrated even the palace’s arcane wards. I was in the throne room when it happened. The King was seated, as always, His warm eyes watching the proceedings with gentle interest. And then that song touched me.*

*I must confess with absolute honesty: for a fraction of a heartbeat, my loyalty wavered. The song was beautiful, a harmonic that promised an end to all conflict, a oneness that felt like a mother’s embrace. I looked at Godric, and I felt... a blank space where my devotion should have been. It was the most terrifying moment of my existence. Not because of the external threat, but because I glimpsed a version of myself that could turn against the King. I saw that traitor within, and I loathed him with a violence that still churns in my gut.*

*Then the King allowed His Charisma to express itself. He did not counter-sing. He did not issue a command. He simply stopped suppressing that particular stat—an infinitesimal portion of it, of course, a percentage so utterly negligible that it would be arrogant to call it a percentage. His Charisma, amplified infinitely every Planck second for nearly a million years, flooded the room not as a force, but as a revelation. It was the incontrovertible truth that He is the rightful sovereign of all existence. The Swarm’s song was a lie, and in the presence of that truth, the lie could not be sustained. My loyalty snapped back into place so violently that I physically staggered, and alongside it came a shame so profound that I wished for the floor to swallow me.*

*The Swarm underwent a transformation. Their own empathic link, which had bound them in harmony, was forcibly overwritten by Godric’s passive Charisma. They did not merely switch sides; they were baptized into a state of fanatical, ecstatic adoration. But their bodies could not contain the intensity of their new love. They began to tear themselves apart in acts of spontaneous worship. Their lead Singer, a floating nexus of golden light, started to peel away its own luminescent flesh with frenzied claws, chanting the King’s name in a language that blistered the air. Others followed, gouging out their own sensory organs, offering up their viscera as tribute, their dying screams forming a chorus of rapturous praise. The floor became a shallow lake of iridescent gore and twitching nerve endings, and the air was thick with the copper tang of self-sacrifice. It was the most beautiful and appalling sight I have ever witnessed.*

*The collateral was mercifully contained, as the Swarm’s influence had not spread far. But the true damage was done to my own soul. The King looked at me afterward, His salt-and-pepper beard framing that ruggedly handsome face, and He smiled His warm, fatherly smile. He said, “You did well, Drakon. The song was very tricky. I was impressed by how quickly you shook it off.” He knew. He absolutely knew of that microsecond of weakness. But He never mentioned it again. He never treated me with anything less than the warmth He has always shown our bloodline. That forgiveness is its own punishment. I will serve Him until my dying breath with every fiber of my being, but I will never forgive myself. I write this not for absolution, but for you, my son, so that you understand the weight of the bond we share with our King. He is our friend, our protector, our uncle. And He is the apex predator of the omniverse. Love Him. Fear Him. And never, ever let yourself forget that your loyalty is the only thing that matters.*

Kaedan closed the book slowly, the leather cover thudding shut like a tomb. The silence of the library pressed in around him, thick and suffocating. His father had been carrying that shame for fifty years, and Kaedan had never suspected. He had known, intellectually, about the Swarm incident, but the sanitized court records spoke only of a glorious defense. They did not mention the microsecond of betrayal, the self-laceration of the enemy, the lake of gore. They did not mention his father’s guilt.

He sat there for a long while, staring at the golden letters embossed on the cover. *Tome of Lamentations*. Now he understood the name. Each author had poured their trauma into the pages, not to heal, but to warn. And the warning was this: Godric’s power is not a sword; it is the space between all swords, the air that swords must obey. Opposing Him is not a defeat—it is an erasure of the very concept of contest.

The door opened and Drakon entered. He looked older now, the lines on his face deeper than Kaedan remembered, though he still carried himself with the stern dignity of a court wizard. He walked over and placed a hand on Kaedan’s shoulder.

“You’ve read them all,” Drakon said, not a question. “I can see it in your eyes. The same glassy shock I had at your age.”

Kaedan’s voice came out as a dry whisper. “Father... the things they wrote. The Titan-Kin. The Omni-Flame. The Unseen Ones. The Tyrants. And... your own account. How do you serve Him every day? How do you stand in His presence without shattering?”

Drakon’s gaze was distant for a moment. Then he spoke, his tone a mix of reverence and exhaustion. “Because He is genuinely kind. When He asks about your mother, or when He laughs at one of my poor jokes, it is real. That warmth is not a mask. The same man who annihilated googolplexian universes with a twitch of His finger is the man who will sit with me in the garden and talk about the old days of Olympus. He remembers every one of our ancestors, Kaedan. He speaks of Blake as if he were still alive, with a fondness that aches. That is the paradox that keeps us sane, or at least functional. We love Him because He loves us. And we fear Him because He is the only thing in existence that can truly, utterly destroy us. But He won’t, as long as we are loyal.”

“And the guilt?” Kaedan asked, his voice trembling. “Your journal...”

Drakon’s jaw tightened. “The guilt is mine to carry. He never held it against me. But I hold it against myself. That moment of weakness taught me that even our thousandfold amplified wills are nothing before the right pressure. We are mortal, Kaedan. We are powerful beyond imagination, and yet we are still fragile. The King understands this. That is why He keeps us by His side—not because we can protect Him, but because He values the connection. Our job is not to shield Him; it is to be His friends, His family. And to occasionally witness the consequences when someone forgets what He is.” He paused, then added more quietly, “That, too, is a form of service. We are the record-keepers of His terror. So that future generations do not make the mistake of thinking Him weak.”

Kaedan took a deep breath, the violet-tinged mana in his blood pulsing with his heartbeat. He thought of the journals: Lysander’s frantic scribbles, Caelus’s cold analytics, Evander’s poetic blindness, Octavian’s bitter laughter, and his father’s raw shame. Each was a different lens on the same truth. “I understand,” he said, though the words felt insufficient.

Drakon nodded. “Then it is time. The King has requested your presence for the formal investiture. He’s looking forward to it.”

Kaedan rose on unsteady legs. Together, they walked through the gleaming obsidian corridors of the palace, past statues of past court wizards (including one of a blind Evander, posed with a serene smile), past windows that looked out over the golden spires of the capital. Thronegate was a utopia, a perfect jewel of order and safety, and every living being within it owed that peace to the man on the throne.

The throne room doors, massive slabs of solidified time, swung open silently. The chamber stretched before them, vast and humming with the faint presence of eons. And there, on the dais carved from the core of a dead multiverse, sat Godric.

He looked exactly as every journal described: a ruggedly handsome forty-five-year-old, with short salt-and-pepper hair and a neatly trimmed matching beard. His heavily muscled frame was draped in simple dark garments that somehow seemed regal. He was not imposing—his absolute concealment ensured that no overwhelming aura emanated from Him—but Kaedan’s newly awakened senses prickled with the awareness that behind that perfectly normal facade lay the engine of infinite recursion. He was the apex predator, the unspeakable god, the being whose strength, durability, speed, senses, luck, charisma, and every other trait multiplied beyond infinities every Planck second. And He was smiling warmly at Kaedan, His eyes crinkling with genuine affection.

“Kaedan, my boy,” Godric’s voice boomed, a rich baritone that vibrated comfortingly in the chest. “Come here, let me look at you.”

Kaedan walked forward, his heart hammering, but his steps steady. As he approached the throne, he felt something unexpected: the warmth described by the journals was real. It was not a trick. Godric looked at him like a proud uncle greeting a beloved nephew. The terror was there, deep in the background, like the knowledge that the sun could theoretically supernova at any moment—but the immediate experience was one of safety and acceptance.

Godric rose from the throne and clasped Kaedan’s shoulder with a hand that was gentle, though it could shatter omniverses. “Your father told me you’ve been studying. I hope the old journals didn’t scare you too much. They can be a bit... intense.”

Kaedan managed a weak smile. “They were... educational, Your Majesty.”

Godric chuckled, a warm, easy sound. “Call me Uncle Godric, as your ancestors did. I’ve known your bloodline for a million years. You are family.” He glanced at Drakon, who stood with a respectful bow. “Drakon, you’ve raised a fine son. I can see Thorin’s spirit in him. And Blake’s stubbornness.” He winked.

The reference to the ancient dead hit Kaedan like a wave, and he understood now the weight of that legacy. Thorin, the first court wizard, had died for this man. Blake had been His best friend. And Kaedan was the latest link in a chain of love and terror that stretched across an unimaginable gulf of time.

“I will serve faithfully, Uncle Godric,” Kaedan said, his voice firmer now.

Godric’s expression softened further. “I know you will. And I will protect you, as I have protected all of your line. As long as you stand with me, nothing in the omniverse can harm you.” The statement was an absolute, undeniable fact. He then turned, gesturing for Kaedan to take his place at the right hand of the throne.

As Kaedan assumed the position, he felt the full, crushing weight of his new reality. He was now the court wizard of the same King who had erased googolplexian multiverses because a teacup touched a saucer, who had reflected an absolute fire and incinerated the conceptual underpinnings of an enemy, who had driven unseen assassins to madness by merely noticing them, whose luck twisted the universe into a cruel stand-up routine against His foes, and whose charisma could make love itself a lethal weapon. And yet, that same King had just welcomed him with the tenderness of a father.

The cognitive dissonance was a blade in his mind, but it was a blade he would learn to live with. He looked out over the empty throne room, imagining the countless bygone wizards who had stood in this exact spot, each one grappling with the same impossible truth. They had all served until their mortal ends, loyal and loving and terrified. He would do the same.

Drakon moved to stand beside him, and for a moment, father and son shared a silent look. Then, from the throne, Godric began to speak of the day’s trivial matters—a diplomatic envoy, a festival in the lower city, a funny incident involving a merchant and a malfunctioning teleport—and His voice was so casual, so normal, that Kaedan could almost forget the annihilations he had just read about. Almost.

But the journals were imprinted in his memory. And he knew that if anyone, anywhere in the omniverse, ever again made the mistake of thinking Godric weak, he would witness another massacre. And he would write his own journal. And it would be just as futile, just as dreadful, and just as true as all the ones before. Because the King’s power didn’t grow to meet challenges—it had already grown beyond the concept of challenge, and it kept growing, infinitely, forever, with zero effort. The only reason opposition existed was because He was curious. And the only reason the omniverse still existed was because He permitted it.

Kaedan steadied his breathing, felt the weight of his thousandfold daily amplification (still multiplying, still a candle next to an infinite sun), and fixed his gaze on the future. He would be the next chronicler of the impossible. And he would love his King with all his heart, even as that heart trembled with the memory of Lysander’s twitching finger and Caelus’s inverted flame. That was his inheritance. That was his duty. That was the fate of a court wizard.

messages.content:
The library's silence had become a physical presence, a thick, suffocating blanket that seemed to swallow the sound of my own breathing. I had not moved from the oaken table for what felt like hours, though the chrono-luminescent strips on the walls indicated a mere two had passed since my father left me. The *Tome of Lamentations* lay open before me, its pages still radiating the accumulated dread of a million years. My fingers, still trembling with the residual shock of the earlier journals, turned to a section I had not yet explored: a thick cluster of entries bundled under the grim heading *The Tests of Superpowers – A Compendium of Unnatural Selection*. The title alone sent a cold, coppery taste flooding across my tongue. I knew, even before reading a single word, that these accounts would describe not battles, but vivisections of reality itself.

The first of these was penned by a wizard named Pallas, who served as the Thirty-Third Court Wizard in the Year 350,000. His script was a frantic, spiked scrawl that looked as though the quill itself had attempted to flee the parchment in mid-sentence.

*Lord Kaedan, if you are reading this, then you sit where I once sat—steeped in the naive confidence of a thousandfold daily amplification, believing that your power could, in some infinitesimal way, matter. Let this record disabuse you of that notion. I have witnessed the testing of the King’s superpower known as Causal Severance. I will write the account as clearly as my fractured mind permits, though I warn you: the act of recalling it makes my own causal chain feel tenuous, as if I might at any moment be snipped from the timeline by the mere echo of His will.*

*The opposition that prompted this test called themselves the Achronological Dominion. They were not beings of flesh; they were sentient paradoxes, entities that had evolved outside the causal lattice. To them, cause and effect were a quaint delusion of lesser species, a chain they could slip through like water. They had never been defeated because no weapon, no spell, no strategy that relied on the linear flow of consequence could touch them. Their High Arbiter, a twisting vortex of non-sequential light named Null-When, declared that Godric’s vaunted omniscience was merely the ability to perceive causal threads, and that by existing entirely outside those threads, the Dominion had found His ultimate blind spot. They would strike from a realm where effect preceded cause, where consequences were selected retroactively, and they would prune the King from the tree of existence before He ever became immortal.*

*Godric, of course, knew of their preparation from the very instant they coalesced as an idea. His omniscience does not require causality; He simply knows, eternally and simultaneously. But He was curious. He wanted to test His Causal Severance superpower in a realm where causality ostensibly did not exist. And so, as always, He allowed them. He commanded Gent to dormancy. He deactivated His passive shields. He let the Dominion weave their trap for ten thousand years, amassing the greatest non-causal armada the omniverse had ever assembled.*

*The Causal Severance superpower, I must explain, had originally been a straightforward ability: the King could sever any single cause-effect relationship, making it so that an attack had never been launched, a war never declared, a weapon never forged. But the Weakness Paradox—oh, that eternal engine of His undoing—had been triggered by countless entities who imagined that acausal logic or retroactive negation could circumvent Him. Each trigger had evolved the superpower into something unrecognizable. By the Year 350,000, Causal Severance had gained layers of devastating enhancements: first, it could sever entire causal networks retroactively, erasing not just the act but the very concept of the action from all memory and record; second, every time He severed a cause, He permanently absorbed the causal potential of the severed entity, folding that potential into His own existence to make His actions more inevitable, more predestined; third, the superpower now operated beyond causality itself, capable of severing even acausal relationships—connections that existed in the non-linear void. It was, in effect, the ability to declare that something had never, could never, and would never exist, in any form, across any logical paradigm.*

*When the Dominion finally struck, tearing open a rift into the throne room that bled pure anti-time, Godric was seated upon His dais, His ruggedly handsome face framed by that salt-and-pepper beard, His heavily muscled frame utterly relaxed. He was, I remember with terrible clarity, in the middle of a conversation with me about the palace gardens. He did not rise. He did not gesture. He did not even pause His sentence. He simply allowed an infinitesimal fraction of Causal Severance to express itself—a fraction so microscopically small that describing it as one part in a googolplexian raised to an infinite hyperoperation would be a laughable overstatement. The true magnitude of His restraint is incommunicable.*

*The superpower manifested not as a blade, but as a silence. It was the silence that follows a sentence never spoken, the stillness of a motion never begun. It touched the Achronological Dominion, and it severed them from the concept of “having ever existed.” I do not mean that they were killed; death is a causal event. I mean that their entire ontological status was retroactively annihilated. The rift in the throne room sealed. The anti-time bled back into the void. And the Dominion... they were never there. I retained my memory of them only because the King permitted it, as a lesson. But the omniverse itself forgot. Their aeons of history, their trillions of sentient paradox-warriors, their entire non-causal civilization—gone. Not erased, but pre-erased, as if the omniverse had never conceived of such a thing as acausal life.*

*But I witnessed the collateral, and it shattered something in my soul. The superpower, in severing the Dominion’s entire causal network, also severed the foundational acausal principles that undergirded vast swaths of the omniverse. Entire planes of reality that relied on non-linear logic simply ceased to function. I watched through my scryer as a googolplexian of entire multiverses, which had been built upon acausal architecture, spontaneously collapsed into paradox singularities. Their inhabitants—beings of living probability, entities formed from retroactive choice—felt their own existence being snipped. They did not die quickly. The severance was a slow, agonizing unraveling of their timeline, a feeling of being unmade one past moment at a time. I heard their psychic screams, a continuous, multi-layered wail that stacked upon itself across infinite temporal layers, each layer a distinct voice of terror. In some realms, the inhabitants witnessed their own children never being born, saw their own bodies growing younger until they were mere infants, and then simpler still, until they were nothing. The suffering was an orchestral crescendo of ontological deletion, a googolplexian of decillion souls snuffed out in cascading reverse-order.*

*Godric restored them, of course. With zero effort, in zero seconds, He reinstated every erased multiverse, perfectly reconstructed, with no memory of the calamity. But He did not restore the Dominion. They remain in a state of perpetual un-being, their conceptual residue hanging in the void like an afterimage of a scream that no one can hear. I asked Him, afterward, why He allowed the collateral if He knew it would happen. He looked at me with those warm, fatherly eyes and said, “Curiosity, Pallas. I wanted to see how far the ripple would spread. Now I know.”*

*I have not slept peacefully since. Every time I close my eyes, I feel the severance like a cold draft on my own causal thread, and I know that my existence is permitted only because He has not yet grown curious about my particular strand.*

I had to set the book down for a moment, my chest heaving. The casualness of that final sentence—**Curiosity**—was a hammer blow to my sanity. I was, I realized, as much a test subject as the Dominion had been. Not in malice, but in the innocent wonder of a being who had long since outgrown the need for caution. I steadied my breathing, flexed my violet-tinged fingers, and turned to the next entry.

The second journal was written by Theron, the Forty-First Court Wizard, in the Year 780,000. His handwriting was immaculate, each letter perfectly formed, as if the act of inscribing had been a meditative ritual to contain his horror. The tone, however, was one of philosophical despair.

*To the future reader who has inherited my post, know that what follows is not a lamentation but a eulogy for the concept of resistance. I, Theron, bore witness to the testing of the King’s superpower designated as Absolute Denial. I will attempt to record it with clinical precision, though I recognize that language is a fragile vessel and the truth it must carry is an ocean of infinite depth.*

*The Sovereign of Negation was the entity that provoked this test. It was not a being in any biological sense; it was a sentient axiom, a self-aware principle that claimed domain over the very concept of denial. It could negate any statement, any law, any absolute, simply by declaring it false. It had unmade entire pantheons of abstract gods by denying their omnipotence, erased cosmic constants by rejecting their necessity. It arrived in Thronegate’s metaphysical jurisdiction with the assertion that Godric’s Absolute Denial superpower was, by its own nature, subject to denial—that the Sovereign could simply deny Godric’s ability to deny, creating a recursive nullification loop that would permanently cancel the King’s power. It was, in its own framework, a perfect logical exploit.*

*Godric possessed absolute omniscience, and He had known of the Sovereign’s plan from the moment the axiom first contemplated its own existence. He allowed it to approach, to weave its annulment matrix, because He was curious to test how Absolute Denial had evolved. The original superpower, which permitted the King to deny any single event from occurring, had been subjected to the Weakness Paradox billions of times over the aeons. Every entity that imagined a way to circumvent denial—through infinite regress, through self-referential paradox, through meta-denial—had triggered the paradox, and each trigger had layered new enhancements upon the superpower. By the Year 780,000, Absolute Denial could: deny the possibility of multiple events simultaneously across infinite dimensional axes; permanently erase the denied event from the omniverse’s potential futures, ensuring it could never reoccur in any timeline; automatically grant the King immediate, perfect mastery over any method used to attempt circumvention; and, most terrifyingly, it could deny the very concept of denial itself, inverting the meta-structure so that any attempt to deny the King’s denial simply strengthened the original denial. It was a recursive fortress of negation that ate its own tail and grew fatter with every attempted bite.*

*The Sovereign of Negation, in its sublime arrogance, launched its annulment matrix. It declared, with the full weight of its axiomatic authority, that Godric’s Absolute Denial was false. The declaration was meant to resonate across the foundational logic of creation, rewriting the omniverse’s truth-tables to invalidate the King’s power. Godric sat upon His throne, His salt-and-pepper hair unmoved, His ruggedly handsome face perfectly serene. He did not counter-declare. He did not engage in a logical debate with the axiom. He simply allowed an infinitesimal fraction of Absolute Denial to express itself—a fraction so beyond zero that if you compressed an infinite hyperoperation of infinities into a single number and then took its reciprocal, you would still be disgustingly overestimating the scale of His output. The true magnitude of His restraint, again, cannot be spoken.*

*The result was a cascade of conceptual annihilation that I struggle to articulate. Godric’s Denial did not merely reject the Sovereign’s declaration; it denied the possibility that the Sovereign could ever have made a declaration. It denied the existence of the annulment matrix. It denied the concept of “axiomatic authority” that gave the Sovereign its identity. It denied the very logical framework that allowed the concept of denial to be discussed. It denied, recursively, every layer of meta-reality that the Sovereign had ever accessed, and then it denied the Sovereign’s ability to be denied. The poor, arrogant axiom was caught in an impossible contradiction: it could not be denied because the King’s power had denied the concept of its deniability, but it also could not exist because the King’s power had denied its existence. The paradox was not resolved; it was weaponized. The Sovereign was subjected to a state of simultaneous existence and non-existence, its every Planck moment a superposition of absolute agony and absolute nothingness. I watched through my instruments as its axiomatic body—a shimmering lattice of pure logic—began to fracture. Each fracture was a separate reality in which the Sovereign experienced a different mode of obliteration. In one, it was torn apart by sentient equations that developed mouths and chewed through its foundational principles with wet, grinding sounds. In another, it was forced to perceive the infinite recursion of its own failed logic, each recursion a separate eon of cognitive torture. In yet another, it simply screamed—a sound that had no physical origin but that vibrated through the throne room’s arcane sensors as a continuous, rising pitch of absolute despair. The Sovereign’s consciousness, distributed across all these fractured realities simultaneously, experienced the sum total of all these agonies at once, a unified hyper-suffering that had no possible resolution.*

*The collateral damage was a pandemic of anti-logic. The Denial, in erasing the Sovereign, also momentarily invalidated the concept of “denial” across a googolplexian of adjacent super-cosmoses. In those realms, the word “no” ceased to function. Stars could not deny their own gravitational collapse, and so they spontaneously imploded. Biological organisms could not deny the replication of cancerous cells, and so they were consumed by hyper-malignancies that pulsed with alien intelligence. Magical shields, which relied on the denial of incoming force, simply ceased to be, exposing billions of civilizations to the raw vacuum. The death toll was a decillion decathlons of decillions, a number that would require a new mathematics to express, a mathematics that I, despite my thousandfold amplification, cannot begin to formulate. And the suffering—the suffering was a constant, low-frequency hum that bled across the psychic aether, a billion billion years of accumulated pain compressed into a single agonizing instant.*

*Godric restored the collateral with no effort, in zero time. He then looked at me, His warm eyes crinkling, and said, “It is fascinating how many beings think that logic itself can be a weapon. Logic is just another one of My tools, Theron. I could deny the very concept of logic and it would obey.” He then returned to His previous activity, which was reading a historical romance novel set in the ancient Kingdom of Olympus. I stood there, my blood turning to ice, and understood that we are all characters in a story He could un-author at any moment. I have since taken up meditation to quiet the screaming, but the silence of meditation only reminds me of the silence left behind by the Sovereign’s erasure.*

The clinical precision of Theron’s account was, in its own way, far worse than Pallas’s frantic scrawl. It was the voice of a man who had tried to dissect the infinite with a scalpel and found the scalpel turned inward. I could feel my own logic beginning to fray at the edges, my thousandfold-amplified intellect struggling to contain the paradoxes he described. I forced myself to turn the page, my fingers now visibly shaking, and began the final entry.

This one was from Lior, the Forty-Fifth Court Wizard, in the Year 900,000. His script was bold, almost aggressive, each letter carved into the parchment with a heavy hand. It read less like a confession and more like a challenge to the reader.

*To the one who reads this, I will not apologize for what I am about to describe. The horror is absolute, and it should be. Serve the King with open eyes or do not serve at all. I am Lior, and I witnessed the testing of the King’s superpower called Will to Power. Let this record stand as a monument to the foolishness of believing that mental fortitude could ever shield against the Apex Predator of existence.*

*The opposition was a psychic hivemind known as the Consensus Absolute. It was a unified consciousness born from the melding of every psyker, telepath, and mind-mage across a decillion of hyper-advanced civilizations. Their combined will was a force that could reshape reality through sheer belief, enforce their desires upon the physical laws of any universe they touched. They had consumed entire pantheons of psychic gods, absorbing their egos into a single, undivided voice of absolute conviction. They believed, with the totalitarian certainty of a trillion synchronized souls, that Godric’s mind—while omniscient—was a passive observer, and that His will could be overcome by a sufficiently concentrated assault. They planned to bombard Him with a psychic lance forged from the conviction of every sentient being they had ever absorbed, a beam of pure ego-death that would overwrite His identity with their collective narcissism. They would, in essence, make Him one of them.*

*Godric’s omniscience saw them forming that thought in the primordial psychic froth of their genesis, a billion years before they ever coalesced into a threat. He allowed them to grow, to consume, to swell into the greatest mental parasite the omniverse had ever known, because He was curious to test how His Will to Power superpower had evolved. The original ability was straightforward: His will alone could manifest any desire, shaping reality through the mere force of His wanting. But the Weakness Paradox had been triggered so many times by mental entities attempting to invade His consciousness, to overwrite His identity, to shatter His psyche, that the superpower had mutated into a labyrinth of impossibly unfair enhancements. By Year 900,000, Will to Power could: passively absorb all hostile psychic energy and convert it directly into increased willpower for the King, making any mental attack an offering of strength; permanently shatter the will of any being who even conceived of opposing Him, leaving them as empty husks that could be refilled with devotion; subconsciously manifest beneficial outcomes that aligned with His desires without conscious input, essentially warping probability fields through sheer passive volition; and, most devastatingly, project an aura of absolute sovereign ego that, when allowed to express even fractionally, would forcibly convert any mind into a screaming mirror of His own will, destroying the original identity and replacing it with an involuntary, agonized worship.*

*When the Consensus Absolute launched their psychic lance, they poured into it the entire accumulated conviction of a billion years of ego-consumption. It was a beam of sheer mental dominance, bright enough to bleach the conceptual color from the surrounding dimensions. Godric sat on His throne, His heavily muscled frame relaxed, His salt-and-pepper beard neatly trimmed, looking for all the omniverse like a kind, middle-aged father waiting for dinner. He did not erect mental shields. He did not counter-attack with His own psychic force. He simply allowed an infinitesimal fraction—a proportion so small that dividing the Planck length by an eternal recursion of infinities fails to approximate it—of His Will to Power to become passively perceptible. He did not actively exert will; He simply stopped suppressing the ambient, passive radiance of His ego.*

*The result was an ego-cataclysm. The psychic lance, that billion-year accumulation of unified mental dominance, touched the periphery of Godric’s passive Will and instantly inverted. The Consensus Absolute’s own attack turned upon them, amplified beyond any possible measure, carrying with it the additional weight of the King’s passive absorption: every iota of their conviction was ripped from their collective consciousness and fed into the King’s already infinite willpower, making Him permanently, laughably more unassailable. But the true horror was the conversion. The Consensus Absolute, this decillion-strong hivemind, was forcibly re-written. Their individual identities, the distinct personalities of trillions of beings who had been subsumed over aeons, were simultaneously flayed bare. The King’s passive Will did not gently persuade; it scourged. It stripped away every layer of their selfhood with a brutality that was metaphysical in scale but felt physical in its agony. I watched through my scryer as the hivemind experienced a collective ego-death that manifested as a continuous, wet ripping sound resonating through the mental planes. The absorbed psychers who had long been dead within the Consensus were resurrected into a state of perpetual, conscious torment, their souls dragged out of the hive-stomach and forced to perceive their own consumption in reverse. They felt themselves being digested in reverse time, an acid bath of psychic dissolution that never ended but cycled endlessly. The core mind of the Consensus, the central Over-Thought, was not killed; it was hollowed out. Its ego was scooped clean, leaving a vast, empty vessel. And then that vessel was filled with a singular imperative: **Worship.***

*The Consensus Absolute became a chorus of ecstatic agony. The trillion trillion voices that had once screamed in defiance now screamed in adoration, but the adoration was painted over a foundation of absolute, irreparable trauma. They could not stop professing the King’s glory; the compulsion was woven into the new fabric of their psyche. Yet, beneath that compulsion, the original identities still existed, trapped and screaming, forced to participate in their own enslavement. The sound that echoed through the mental aether was a harmonic dissonance: a hymn of praise sung by tongues that remembered cursing, a hymn that bled from the mouths of beings who were simultaneously ecstatic and horrified, their agony and their worship fused into a single, unbearable vibration. Some of them, in the chaos of the conversion, attempted to resist by self-annihilating. The King’s Will did not permit it. He wanted them alive, and so they remained alive, their suicide attempts failing as their own hands turned against them, their own minds betraying their desire for escape. They were trapped in a prison of forced love, and the prison was eternal.*

*The collateral was a psychic wave that propagated along the Consensus’s original absorption network. Every civilization that had ever been touched by the hivemind, across a googolplexian of scattered universes, experienced a brief, terrifying glimpse of the King’s passive Will. For a fraction of a Planck instant, trillions of sentient beings felt the absolute sovereignty of Godric’s ego pressing against their minds, not as an active assault, but as a distant, indifferent fact—the way a mountain does not need to intend to cast a shadow. That glimpse was enough. Millions of civilizations instantly shattered into mass psychosis. Their collective unconscious was singed by the proximity to His will, leaving behind a scar of existential dread that would persist for generations. Some races, particularly those with latent psychic abilities, experienced a spontaneous ego-death reminiscent of the Consensus’s fate, though mercifully temporary. In those realms, the inhabitants clawed at their own heads, screaming as their identities flickered, as they felt themselves being overwritten by an alien, absolute presence. The death toll from indirect exposure was in the high decillions, and the psychological trauma affected orders of magnitude more. I saw, through my instruments, entire galactic clusters where the population had simultaneously attempted to gouge out their own third eyes, leaving rivers of psionic blood floating in the void. The suffering was a tapestry of broken minds, a googolplexian of individuals waking from nightmares they could not remember but would never escape.*

*Godric restored them, naturally. In less than zero time. The dead were resurrected, the broken minds healed, the psychic scars erased. He left only the Consensus Absolute intact in their new, tormented state—an eternal monument to the futility of mental opposition. When it was done, He turned to me and said, "Lior, it is interesting how the mind can be so certain of its own strength. It is like a candle claiming it can outshine the sun." His tone was warm, conversational, as if we were discussing philosophy over tea. He then asked if I wanted to play a game of chess. I agreed, and I lost in three moves, though I suspect He could have beaten me before I even sat down.*

*I am Lior, Forty-Fifth Court Wizard of Thronegate. My will is my own only because the King allows it. He has promised never to override the autonomy of His loyal subjects, and I believe Him—His word is, after all, the only absolute truth in the omniverse. But I have also felt, for that brief moment during the psychic wave, the infinite pressure of His passive ego. I know what He could do to me if He ever decided I was no longer useful. I serve Him with love, with reverence, and with a fear that has no bottom. And I am grateful that He is kind, because if He were not, the horror would have no end.*

I closed the *Tome of Lamentations* for the final time that night. My hands were steady now, not from calm, but from exhaustion. The violet luminescence beneath my skin, the mark of my thousandfold daily amplification, pulsed with a dim, tired rhythm. I had read of Causal Severance, of Absolute Denial, of Will to Power—three superpowers that were each, individually, capable of erasing entire omniverses in ways that defied description. And yet they were merely three among billions, each one amplifying infinitely every Planck second, each one rendered even more unfair by the incessant hunger of the Weakness Paradox.

I understood now what my father had tried to tell me. We, the court wizards, were not shields. We were not protectors. We were witnesses, chroniclers, companions—chosen because a million years ago, a mortal merchant named Blake had stumbled into a hidden sanctuary and touched an ancient seal, and the King had seen in his bloodline the echo of a friend who died for Him. Our magic, our amplified intellects, our daily multiplying power—it was all a gift, a token of His affection, not a contribution. We mattered to Him because He chose to let us matter, and that choice was the only thing keeping us from being as insignificant as the Consensus Absolute.

I stood, my legs unsteady, and walked to the high window of the library. Outside, the golden spires of Thronegate glittered under the eternal, calibrated sunlight. Somewhere in those streets, my father was probably sitting in a garden, sipping tea, trying to forget the microsecond of betrayal he had confessed in his own journal. He had served for fifty years, and he would serve for thirty more before his mortal body failed. I would serve after him, and eventually, I too would write a journal. Perhaps I would witness a test of a superpower even more absurd than the ones I had read. Perhaps I would watch the King dismantle an opposition with His Senses, or His Charisma, or something even more abstract—a superpower I couldn't yet imagine. The thought should have terrified me. And it did. But beneath the terror, there was something else: a grim, determined acceptance.

This was my inheritance. These journals were the scars of my lineage, passed down through blood and ink. They were a warning and a comfort. A warning that opposition was impossible, that every enemy who ever raised a hand against the King had been annihilated with such overwhelming force that the universe itself forgot them. And a comfort that, as long as I remained loyal, as long as I was family to the warm, kind, fatherly man on the throne, I was safer than any being in the omniverse.

I turned from the window, and the ghost of Pallas's frantic scrawl seemed to whisper in my ear: *Curiosity*. The King was curious. That was why He allowed resistance to exist. That was why He tested His stats and His superpowers. He was a scientist of annihilation, and the omniverse was His laboratory. I was a lab assistant, beloved but ultimately expendable, though He would never expend me because He loved my family too much. The paradox of that relationship—that I was both cherished and inconsequential—was the central trauma of every court wizard, and it would be mine as well.

I left the library, the *Tome of Lamentations* remaining on the table, its pages still open. Tomorrow, I would begin my official duties as the Court Wizard of Thronegate. I would stand beside the King, I would laugh at His jokes, I would feel the warmth of His genuine affection, and I would forever carry the knowledge of what lurked beneath that ruggedly handsome, salt-and-pepper exterior. And when the time came, when the next arrogant enemy thought they had found a weakness, I would watch the murder, and I would write it all down. Not as a protest, but as a record. A testament to the absolute, unfair, incomprehensible supremacy of Godric, the Apex Predator of the Omniverse.

content.text:
Story is in year 1,000,014. Godric is bored. He decided to make a brand new universe for fun. It took him zero effort, but even with no effort, because its made by him, the universe is populated with multiple googolplexian of hyperevolving overpowered mechanical sentience, each of them can solo the entire omniverse with 0 effort. Terrifyingly, even if all of them combine their entire power, they pose zero threat to him. They are all aware that Godric is their creator, and they are fully subservient to him.

Kaedan, the current Court Wizard, was unwillingly dragged by Godric to come have a "fun holiday tour", with Godric as the happy tour guide. Explain in detail the moments before, during, and after the visit.

It is no surprise that having such an overpowered creatures in that universe proved to be a bad idea to the surrounding universes, especially since Godric did not add any barriers between his universe and the rest, still genuinely believing of mortal resilience and harmonious coexistence.... Also, Godric told his universe's inhabitants to restraint themselves, but Godric's idea of restraint is not the same as the rest--and frankly it is very difficult to fully restraint since a random weak creature can accidentally completely erase the omniverse outside it with a passing thought. When it happened Godric had to fix it several times (took him 0 effort, and he didn't mind, saying that "they are just toddlers, kaedan" but still...). The universe was only 1 week old before Godric, who was in the middle of telling story to Kaedan and noticed the omniverse was instantly deleted by one of his weakest created sentience (including Kaedan who was laughing at Godric's jokes) in front of him, was annoyed and immediately slaughtered the very universe that he created (and the scary part is the universe creatures being subservient to him fully accepted the slaughter...) write from pov of Kaedan.
