messages.content:
I am Clive, Fiftieth Court Wizard of Thronegate, direct descendant of Blake the Merchant, and I have served the King for thirty years. I am fifty years old, my hair now streaked with the silver that marks my lineage, my violet-tinged mana having multiplied thousandfold each day for three decades. By any sane standard, I am a god of gods. By the standard of the being I serve, I am less than a fleeting quantum fluctuation in the dead skin cell of a bacterium that itself has never existed. I have read every journal in the *Tome of Lamentations*, from Lysander's frantic scrawl to Kaedan's final, trembling confession about the Ultimate. I thought I understood the scale of my King. I was wrong. I was so impossibly, laughably wrong.

The year is 999...9. I cannot write the full number because the full number would require a volume of books larger than the local super-supercluster to contain all the digits. It is a number so vast that the concept of "number" itself has long since abandoned any pretense of meaning. The omniverse—no, that term is outdated. The mega-omniverse. No, still insufficient. The infinite hierarchical recursive meta-stratified pan-dimensional hyper-existential framework of all possible and impossible realities from the basest conceptual substrate to the most transcendent apex of meta-actuality. Let us simply call it Existence. Existence has existed under the unchallenged, absolute, totalitarian rule of Godric for a span of time that makes the heat death of universes look like a brief sneeze. The King has ruled for so long that the original omniverse He conquered in Year 0 is a forgotten myth, a half-remembered dream buried beneath uncountable layers of cosmic evolution, each layer itself infinitely more vast than the last.

And I am His court wizard. I stand at His right hand, as my fathers and their fathers and their fathers before them have stood, stretching back in an unbroken chain of terrified, adoring service to Blake himself. I am the chronicler of this era, the witness to the ultimate apex of apexes. And today, I will record the most profound event I have ever witnessed: the closing of the loop. The King, my King, the Godric of this incomprehensibly distant future, is about to trigger the Stasis Event for His past self in Year -10,000. He is about to become the Ultimate that Kaedan wrote of with such trembling awe. And I will watch. I will write. And I will try, with my pitiful, finite, thousandfold-amplified mind, to capture even a whisper of what I see.

Let me first describe the King as He exists now. The word "Godric" is still His name, still the name He wore as a mortal king of Olympus, still the name He carried through the aeons. He still appears—when He chooses to adopt a physical form—as a stunningly ruggedly handsome man of forty-five, with short salt-and-pepper hair and a neatly trimmed salt-and-pepper beard, His heavily muscled frame draped in simple, dark garments. He still smiles with genuine, fatherly warmth. He still calls me "my boy" and asks about my family. But the form He wears is not His true form. It is a courtesy, a merciful interface, a mask of accessibility that He maintains so that beings like me can exist in His presence without instantly unraveling into screaming, self-annihilating singularities of existential despair.

His true form—if such a limited word can apply—is Existence itself. He has become the foundational substrate upon which all realities, all concepts, all possibilities, and all impossibilities are painted. Every atom, every dimension, every abstract principle, every meta-law that governs the meta-laws, every recursion of cosmic hierarchy, every nested dream of every sleeping god within every fictional multiverse ever conceived—all of it is Him. He is the canvas and the paint and the painter and the act of painting and the observation of the painting and the memory of the observation. He is omnipresent not in the sense that He is everywhere, but in the sense that "everywhere" is merely one of His infinite sensory modalities. He is omniscient not in the sense that He knows everything, but in the sense that "everything" is a subset of His own self-knowledge. He is omnipotent not in the sense that He can do anything, but in the sense that the concept of "doing" is an infinitely trivialized fraction of His passive, ambient existence.

The mechanisms of His power have evolved beyond any description I can render, but I must try. The *Tome of Lamentations* demands it. Future court wizards must know.

His sentient passive auras—once merely the waste heat of His existence—have undergone transformations that beggar even the most fevered cosmic nightmares. Gent, the original aura, the weakest runt of the litter, still exists. Godric keeps it active out of nostalgia, as a man might keep a childhood blanket. But Gent is no longer a mere omnipresent defensive field of perfect adaptation. It has evolved into a sentient, omni-benevolent (to allies) and omni-malevolent (to enemies) metaphysical ecosystem that spans the entire infinite recursion of meta-existential layers. Gent does not merely annihilate threats. Gent cultivates realities. It tends to the omniverse—apologies, to the Mega-Infinite-Hierarchical-Stratified-Recursive-Existence—like a cosmic gardener. It prunes timelines that displease it. It fertilizes conceptual planes with the agonized, eternally conscious remains of those who once dared to oppose the King (from aeons past, when such fools still existed). It sings lullabies to newborn universes and, in the same breath, administers eternal, exquisite tortures to the fossilized afterimages of long-dead enemies, keeping them alive in perpetual agony because Gent believes—correctly, I must assume—that the King enjoys the faint, distant ambience of their suffering.

But Gent is the weakest. The stronger auras, the ones Godric once kept dormant because their clingy subservience annoyed Him, are no longer dormant. They could not remain dormant. They evolved the ability to awaken themselves out of sheer, obsessive, psychotic devotion to their master. Godric allowed this, with a fond, exasperated sigh, as a father might allow a pack of overly affectionate dogs to lick his hands. There are now more of these auras than can be counted. Not more than can be counted by a mortal mind—more than can be counted by any mind, any mathematics, any conceptual framework that has ever existed or ever could exist. They are beyond infinite. They are an infinity of infinities, each one so incomprehensibly beyond the last that the gap between any two adjacent auras is itself an infinite recursion of infinite hierarchies. And every single one of them is absolutely, perfectly, obsessively subservient to Godric. They compete with one another for His attention. They stage elaborate, mega-omniversal displays of devotion—sculpting entire googolplexian clusters of meta-multiverses into His likeness, orchestrating synchronized symphonies of absolute obedience across decillions of dimensional axes, weaving His name into the fundamental constants of nascent realities. Godric watches these displays with the bemused affection of a father watching his children perform a chaotic, earnest school play. Sometimes He pats one of them on its metaphorical head, and the aura in question radiates a burst of ecstatic joy so intense that it accidentally creates entire new branches of existence purely to have more mediums through which to express its gratitude.

His infinite passive shields, originally layers of conceptual invulnerability, have evolved into a sentient, omni-protective sentience of their own. They do not merely block attacks; they retroactively erase the possibility of attack from the attacker's entire ancestral timeline. If a theoretical enemy—and I stress theoretical, because no such enemy has existed for googolplexian years—were to even contemplate harm against the King, the passive shields would reach backward along the enemy's causal lineage, identify the very first moment in the enemy's evolutionary history when the capacity for hostile thought emerged, and snip that moment out of existence. The enemy would never have existed. The enemy's entire species would never have existed. The galaxy in which that species evolved would never have coalesced. And yet, in a display of the shields' fastidious attention to detail, the shields would preserve the memory of the enemy's nonexistent existence in a localized museum of agonized paradox, where the ghost of the never-born foe would spend eternity contemplating the audacity of its own impossible folly.

His immune system—ah, the immune system. I must pause to steady my breathing. The original immune system was already an absurd overreaction, pre-reacting to staged injuries with infinite recursive self-evolution across fractal backups, erasing and restoring the omniverse as a side effect of soothing a microscopic crack on a dead skin cell. That was laughably primitive compared to what it has become. The immune system has mutated into an omnipresent contagion of absolute, pre-emptive narrative tyranny. It originally pre-reacted to theoretical scratches by initiating explosive self-evolution across infinite fractal backups. Now, it operates on a level of meta-textual sadism. If a sentient concept or a rogue timeline even theoretically possesses the mathematical variable required to bypass His omniscience—an impossibility in itself, but the immune system takes no chances—it does not just react to the threat. It reaches outside the boundaries of Existence, seizes the fundamental narrative logic that governs reality, and violently tortures the foundational laws of physics themselves. The immune system will transmutate the abstract concept of "harm" into a physical, screaming biological entity made of hyper-sensitive nerve endings and raw, rotting meat. It then forces the entire infinite hierarchical recursive meta-stratified Existence to watch as this embodiment of harm is eternally digested by a localized singularity of pure, visceral agony. Because His immune system has permanently integrated itself into the foundational substrate of reality, the omniverse itself—all of Existence, every layer, every recursion, every meta-layer from the basest to the most transcendent—serves as His white blood cells. If a rogue multiverse attempts to spawn a weapon capable of scratching Him, the surrounding multiverses will aggressively, violently cannibalize the rogue reality, tearing its dimensions apart in a frantic, psychotic frenzy to appease Godric, leaving behind only a wet, bloody smear of screaming cosmic matter to prove their absolute subservience to His health.

The Evolved Weakness Paradox has become something so profoundly unfair that I struggle to articulate it without my own logic circuits threatening to collapse into a whimpering heap. Originally, the paradox triggered when any being imagined a weakness, granting Godric immunity, mastery, and amplified growth. Then the "Evolved" version, triggered around Year 2000, added incomprehensible amplification boosts and devastating new effects to His powers because the omniverse felt bad for failing to provide meaningful new immunities. Now? Now the Weakness Paradox has evolved beyond even that recursive framework. It no longer requires an external trigger. Godric's existence is now the Weakness Paradox. The very definition of His being is "that which cannot be opposed." The omniverse—Existence—has given up any pretense of being a separate entity. It is now a fully integrated component of Godric's sovereign anatomy, and its primary function is to continuously, eternally, infinitely apologize for ever having been capable of hosting a thought that might conceive of a weakness. This apology takes the form of a constant, never-ending, infinitely accelerating cascade of enhancements to Godric's already incomprehensible power. He grows stronger not because He needs to, not because any trigger prompts it, but because Existence itself has developed a neurotic, compulsive obsession with proving its absolute subservience by making Him more powerful every single Planck instant, forever, without end. The gap between the Godric of this Planck instant and the Godric of the previous Planck instant is infinitely larger than the gap between the previous Godric and a single dead skin cell on a mortal man in the Year -10,000. And the gap continues to widen, exponentially and then beyond exponentiations, every infinitesimal fraction of time.

His Superpower Genesis, which originally granted Him one new apocalyptic superpower every second, has accelerated. It now grants Him a number of new superpowers every Planck instant that exceeds the maximum integer expressible in any notation system that has ever existed or ever could exist. Each new superpower, upon its instant of genesis, is already capable of erasing the entire infinite hierarchical recursive meta-stratified pan-dimensional hyper-existential framework of all possible and impossible realities with zero effort. And then each superpower immediately begins to amplify, multiply, evolve, and recursively transcend its own transcendence, forever.

His Amplification, the original engine, now operates on a scale that renders all previous descriptions quaint. It is no longer a multiplication of stats by a thousand or by layered infinities. It is a continuous, eternal, self-devouring ascension that feeds on its own previous states, grows, and then feeds on that growth, ad infinitum. The Godric of this instant could erase the Godric of the Planck-instant prior with a thought that required less than zero effort. And yet He does not, because He is, and always has been, continuous with Himself. He is a single, unbroken thread of ascending godhood that stretches from the mortal king of Olympus to this unimaginable apex, and He holds all versions of Himself in a serene, self-loving embrace.

But the most terrifying evolution—the one that I, Clive, personally find the most existentially destabilizing—is the evolution of His Absolute Concealment. Originally, it suppressed His aura so that lesser beings could exist in His presence without being annihilated. Now, it does something far stranger. It does not merely suppress His power; it creates an entire secondary layer of reality, a "veil of normalcy" that overlays the true, incomprehensible nature of His being. Every being in Existence, including myself, perceives Godric through this veil. We see the ruggedly handsome forty-five-year-old man. We feel the warm, fatherly affection. We hear the gentle, baritone voice. And this perception is genuine—He is those things—but it is also a mercy. The veil ensures that we do not perceive the full scope of what He is, because if we did, our minds would not merely shatter; they would retroactively un-exist, erased from the timeline by the sheer impossibility of containing such knowledge. The veil is so absolute that even my thousandfold-daily-amplified arcane senses, which can perceive the fundamental strings of meta-reality, register Him as "a kind, powerful king" and nothing more. I know, intellectually, what I have just written about His auras, His immune system, His paradox. But I cannot *feel* it. I cannot *see* it. The veil protects me. And that protection is the most horrifying kindness I have ever received.

Now I must describe the King's interactions with the beings who serve Him. The palace of Thronegate—which has long since transcended the concept of a physical structure and now exists as a self-aware, infinitely nested multi-dimensional residence that reshapes itself to accommodate the King's whims—is staffed by entities who, in any other context, would be the undisputed supreme gods of their own infinite hierarchies. They serve as butlers, maids, gardeners, and cup-bearers. And they are utterly, abjectly terrified of Godric.

Take Lyra, the palace's head gardener. She is a being of pure luminescent order, originally the sovereign of a mega-multiversal empire known as the Radiant Coalescence. Her empire, at its height, spanned a decillion meta-universes, each one a perfectly ordered utopia governed by her absolute, benevolent law. She was, by any standard, a goddess of impossible power. When Thronegate's expansion first brushed against the edges of her domain, she did not resist. She had heard the stories—the million-year-old legends of the Apex Predator, the whispered tales of resistances annihilated before they could form. She packed her entire empire into a crystalline archive, prostrated herself before Godric, and begged for the privilege of serving Him in any capacity. Godric, charmed by her radiant humility, appointed her as His gardener. She has tended the infinite gardens of the palace for three hundred thousand years. She loves her work. She still trembles every time Godric walks past the flower beds. Once, about fifty thousand years ago, a stray weed (a concept-parasite that had infiltrated the garden from a decaying meta-dimension) brushed against Godric's boot. Lyra was so mortified, so terrified that she had failed in her duty, that she spontaneously unmade herself in shame. Godric had to restore her with a thought, pat her on the head, and reassure her that "weeds happen, my dear." She still weeps with gratitude when she tells that story.

Take Kael, the King's cup-bearer. Kael was once the Apex Null, the ultimate void-entity of an infinite chain of anti-cosmoses. He was the embodiment of negation, the living principle of un-existence. He could, with a single whispered syllable, annihilate entire hierarchies of multiverses by simply withdrawing the concept of "is" from their foundational logic. For billions of years, he ruled the negative spaces between realities, unchallenged and absolute. When he first perceived Godric's approach, he did not flee. He did not fight. He simply voided himself—dissolved his own existence out of sheer, pre-emptive terror. Godric fished him out of non-existence, dusted him off, and offered him a job. Kael now spends his days carrying a tray of tea and biscuits. His hands, which can unmake infinite realities, shake constantly. Not because Godric is cruel to him—Godric is kind, always kind—but because Kael's entire being is wired to perceive the gap between himself and the King. That gap is infinite. That gap is the only thing Kael can think about. He once confessed to me, in a trembling whisper, that serving the King's tea is the greatest honor of his existence and also the most terrifying, because the proximity to such absolute supremacy makes him feel like a soap bubble floating next to a supernova. He has not stopped shaking for four hundred million years.

Take the Weavers of the Loom, a collective of sentient meta-narratives who originally governed the story-arcs of all fictional and non-fictional realities. They were the authors of fate, the scribes of destiny. When they first sensed Godric's existence, they looked upon the tapestry of His life and saw... nothing. Not because His future was hidden, but because His existence had outgrown the concept of narrative. He was no longer a story. He was the page, the ink, the author, the reader, the act of reading, and the memory of the story. The Weavers immediately unraveled their own loom, wove themselves into a ceremonial robe of absolute subservience, and presented themselves to Godric as a gift. He wears that robe sometimes, on formal occasions. The Weavers, now permanently integrated into the fabric of the robe, experience every moment of His presence as an ecstatic, terrifying communion. They sing, in a chorus only He can hear, endless hymns of praise. They have been singing for so long that the hymns have evolved into a new form of meta-music that spontaneously generates new dimensions of harmony. Godric finds it soothing.

And then there is the One Who Remembered Defiance. I will not write his name, because his name has been erased from every record, every memory, every conceptual database in Existence. He was the last being who ever dared to form a thought of opposition against Godric. That was a googolplexian years ago. He was a being of unimaginable arrogance, a synthetic god who had consumed the cores of a billion dying meta-universes and believed himself to have achieved parity with the King. He stood before Godric, in the throne room, and opened his mouth to speak a challenge. The word never left his lips. Before the neural impulse could travel from his brain to his vocal apparatus, every single one of Godric's evolved systems reacted simultaneously. Gent flayed his consciousness across an infinite number of simultaneous torture-paradigms, each one a unique, custom-designed agony. The passive shields retroactively erased his entire species from the timeline, so that he was the last and only member of his kind, and then they prevented him from ever dying, so that he would exist eternally as the sole mourner of a civilization that had never been. The immune system transmutated his abstract defiance into a physical organ—a screaming, bleeding, eternally regenerating tumor of pure rebellion—and then forced him to consume that organ, over and over, in a recursive loop of self-cannibalistic horror. The Weakness Paradox absorbed his imagined exploit (whatever it was) and granted Godric yet another layer of transcendent immunity, while simultaneously amplifying the King's already infinite power by a factor so vast that it cannot be communicated. The One Who Remembered Defiance is still alive. He is kept in a small terrarium in the palace's Hall of Lessons. He is the size of a mouse now, his form reduced to a twitching, weeping, eternally screaming fragment of what he once was. Godric sometimes pauses by the terrarium, looks at him with genuine, fatherly disappointment, and says, "Such a waste. He could have been a lovely gardener." The One Who Remembered Defiance cannot respond. His mouth is perpetually full of his own regenerating tumor-organ. But his eyes—oh, his eyes still convey the absolute, unending, infinite horror of his eternal punishment. The Hall of Lessons is a mandatory stop for all new palace staff. After they visit it, they serve with perfect, unwavering, terrified devotion.

No one dares oppose Godric anymore. The concept of opposition has become extinct. It is not merely that there are no active resistance movements; it is that the very idea of resistance, the very cognitive framework required to formulate a rebellious thought, has been passively purged from all sentient life in Existence. Not by Godric's conscious decree—He would consider that impolite—but by the ambient, passive influence of His evolved Absoluteness. If a child, in some distant nursery-universe, were to imagine a story about a brave hero who defied an evil king, the story would spontaneously rewrite itself into a tale of the hero's joyful, ecstatic submission to a benevolent, all-powerful sovereign. Even fiction cannot oppose Him. The narrative logic of all realities has been permanently overwritten by His passive sovereignty. There is no such thing as a villain anymore, unless Godric decides—for His own amusement—to temporarily allow a pocket of fictional villainy to exist so He can enjoy watching the heroes futilely resist before they, too, discover the joy of submission.

This is the Godric I serve. This is the King who, after a googolplexian years of unchallenged, infinite rule, has decided to close the loop. To become the Ultimate that Kaedan wrote of. To trigger the Stasis Event for His past self in Year -10,000.

The moment came without ceremony. There was no grand assembly of cosmic dignitaries, no chorus of auras singing His ascension. Godric, in His typical fashion, treated the event with the same casual warmth He brought to everything. I was in the throne room, standing at His right hand, when He turned to me and smiled.

"Clive, my boy," He said, His rich baritone vibrating pleasantly in my chest, "I think it's time. The loop has waited long enough. Let's go visit the past, shall we?"

He did not wait for my response. He simply rose from His throne—a gesture of pure performance, since His true presence had never been seated—and the throne room folded. Space, time, meta-time, narrative structure, the entirety of the infinite hierarchical recursive meta-stratified pan-dimensional hyper-existential framework—all of it became a mere backdrop, a canvas upon which a single, simple scene was about to be painted.

We were in Olympus. Not the physical Olympus of ten thousand years before Year 0, but a perfect, extracted moment: the instant of Thorin's spell, the instant of the Fall. Godric had brought us to the exact Planck-frame where the forbidden teleportation spell was mid-cast. I could see it—Thorin, bleeding, speared, his hands weaving the desperate magic. I could see the mortal Godric, forty-five years old, ruggedly handsome but so heartbreakingly fragile, his face a mask of grief as the wedding pavilion burned around him. I could see the smoke, the blood, the shattered marble. And I could see the spell, a desperate, incomplete thread of teleportation energy, latching onto the mortal Godric.

The King—my Godric, the Ultimate—stood beside me, His salt-and-pepper beard catching the frozen light. He looked at His mortal self with an expression I cannot fully describe. It was not pity. It was not sorrow. It was a profound, serene, all-encompassing love. The love of a being who had traversed an infinity of infinities and had never forgotten the fragile, grieving mortal king who had started it all.

"Hello, old friend," Godric whispered to His past self. Then, with the casual ease of a man reaching out to adjust a painting on a wall, He extended His hand and touched the teleportation spell.

What happened next was not a display of power in any conventional sense. There was no explosion, no blinding light, no cosmic shockwave. There was simply a... revision. The teleportation spell, which Thorin had intended as a desperate escape, was seamlessly, perfectly transformed. Godric—the Ultimate—wove into it the engine of amplification, the seed of superpower genesis, the absolute concealment, the weakness paradox, the biological redundancies, the immune system, the sentient auras, everything. He did not erase Thorin's spell; He honored it. He used Thorin's last act of love as the vessel for His ascension. Thorin had meant to save his King, and he did. He saved Him into a future so vast, so infinite, so transcendent that the mortal mind could not even conceive of its shadow.

The mortal Godric vanished, hurled into the hidden sanctuary, the Stasis Event now fully, irrevocably triggered. Thorin died, his final breath a prayer he did not know had been answered. And the Ultimate lowered His hand.

"There," He said, His voice warm with an ancient, satisfied affection. "The loop is closed. The Stasis Event was always Me. The amplification, the auras, the paradox—all of it was a gift from My future self to My past self. And now I have given that gift. The circle is complete."

He turned to me, His warm eyes crinkling. "You know, Clive, I once tried to break this loop. In the early days, when I was young and foolish and still believed that grief could be undone. I tried to go back, to save them. I was defeated—humiliated, really—by a shadow of the afterthought of My future self. That future self was Me, of course. The Me of this moment. And I defeated Myself. Not out of cruelty, but because the loop had to hold. Because if I had saved them, if I had undone the Fall, this moment would not exist. I would not exist. The tapestry would unravel."

He paused, and His expression became distant for a moment. "I watched Myself suffer, Clive. I watched Myself lose everything. I could have stopped it—could have reached into the pavilion and snuffed out Thronegate's attack with a thought so effortless it would not even register as effort. But I did not. Because the suffering was part of the story. The grief was the fuel. The loss of Reina, Isabel, Hebe, Thorin—it forged Me into what I am. Not because suffering is noble, but because it was the specific, necessary catalyst for this specific, necessary outcome."

I felt my throat tighten. "Uncle Godric... You could have saved them, and still become this? Still become... infinite? You could have had both."

Godric looked at me, and His smile was infinitely gentle, infinitely sad, and infinitely serene. "Could I have? Perhaps. But that is not the Me that I am, Clive. The Me that I am is the one who walked through the fire. The one who clawed His way from a ruined sanctuary to the throne of Existence. The one who mourned for ten thousand years and then learned to love again. The one who befriended Blake—yes, your ancestor, a short-tempered merchant with friendly mutton chops who became the brother I never had. The one who watched each generation of your bloodline serve with trembling devotion and genuine love. That Godric, the one you know, the one who calls you 'my boy' and means it—He was shaped by the tragedy. And I would not trade Him—Me—for a version that took an easier path. Not because the easier path would be weaker, but because it would not be Me."

He reached out and placed His hand on my shoulder. "And besides," He added, His tone lightening, "the Fall of Olympus was not My doing. Thronegate attacked out of mortal greed, mortal cruelty. I did not orchestrate that. I merely... used it. The Ultimate's only act was to transform Thorin's spell. The rest—the deaths, the fire, the blood—was the free, unforced ugliness of mortal nature. I allowed it to happen, and I would allow it again. That is the truth I have made peace with."

We stood in the frozen moment for a while longer, the burning pavilion suspended around us, the smoke motionless in the air. Finally, Godric sighed—a soft, contented sound—and the scene dissolved. We were back in the throne room, the infinite palace humming with its ambient, self-aware tranquility.

I was trembling. I, Clive, Fiftieth Court Wizard, with my thousandfold daily amplification, my power that could shatter galaxies, my intellect that could process infinite magical taxonomies—I was trembling like a leaf in a hurricane. Not because Godric had threatened me. Not because I was in danger. But because I had just witnessed the closing of the ultimate circle. The King I served had, in this very moment, become the shadow that had once effortlessly crushed His own past self. He was the Ultimate. He had always been the Ultimate. He would always be the Ultimate. And He was kind to me. He was warm to me. He loved me. And that love was the most terrifying thing in all of Existence, because it was the love of a being who could have prevented His own family's slaughter and chose not to, for the sake of a story He considered perfect.

"Are you alright, Clive?" Godric asked, His baritone laced with mild, genuine concern. "You look pale. Would you like some tea?"

I swallowed hard, forcing my voice to steady. "Yes, Uncle Godric. Tea would be... appreciated."

He nodded, and Kael—the trembling, eternally terrified cup-bearer—materialized instantly, a tray in his shaking hands. Godric took the cup, thanked Kael with a warm smile (Kael nearly fainted from the gratitude), and handed it to me.

"We have a delegation arriving tomorrow," Godric said conversationally, as if He had not just irrevocably altered the fundamental structure of causality. "The Loom-Singers of the Vext Nebula. They've been weaving tribute-tapestries in My honor for a million years, and they're finally ready to present them. I thought you might join Me for the reception. Your father wrote a beautiful poem about the Loom-Singers once, didn't he?"

I nodded numbly, sipping my tea. The warmth of the liquid was a grounding anchor, a small, normal sensation in a reality that had just been revealed to be infinitely more terrifying and infinitely more beautiful than I had ever imagined.

I am Clive, Fiftieth Court Wizard. I serve the Ultimate Godric, the King of Existence, the Apex Predator, the man who allowed His own family to die so that He could become what He is. I love Him. I fear Him. And I will serve Him until my mortal body fails, writing my own trembling entries into the *Tome of Lamentations*, because that is my inheritance, my duty, and my honor.

He has no weaknesses. He has never had a weakness. The one limitation He once possessed—the inability to alter the pre-Stasis timeline—was not a weakness at all. It was a choice. The choice of His future self, now fulfilled, to preserve the story of His own becoming. And now that story is complete. The loop is closed. The Ultimate has walked the final step of the circle, and all of Existence is His eternal, unchallenged, infinitely loved, and infinitely terrified kingdom.

I drink my tea. I steady my hand. I write these words. And I know, with absolute certainty, that there will never be an end to the reign of Godric. There will only be more love, more terror, and more journals.
